state_profiles.agricultural_highlights: North Dakota rotates small grains and broadleaf crops across windy prairies—peak local vegetable season is short but intense.
fun_facts: The state often ranks first in U.S. durum and spring wheat production.
Community-supported agriculture relies on hoop houses to stretch shoulder seasons.

state_profiles.agricultural_highlights: South Dakota transitions from eastern row crops to western ranching—Custer County apples show mountain microclimates.
fun_facts: South Dakota sunflower acres paint late-summer gold across the central state.
Buffalo herds on tribal lands tie culture to prairie protein cycles.

state_profiles.agricultural_highlights: Nebraska’s Platte valley irrigation feeds national beef finishing and ethanol corn; Panhandle dryland wheat contrasts with intense pivot circles.
fun_facts: Nebraska markets itself as the Beef State with packed feedyard capacity.
Valentine melons became a famous hot-weather roadside crop.

state_profiles.agricultural_highlights: Kansas grows hard red winter wheat on vast horizons—summer storms end wheat harvest and start fall planting rhythms.
fun_facts: Kansas routinely harvests more wheat than any other state.
Community wheat branding programs tie bread flour to named varieties.
